title: Chuck JonesтАЩ The Dot and the Line Celebrates Geometry & Hard Work: An Oscar-Winning Animation (1965)
description:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ani┬нmat┬нed short above, The Dot and the Line, direct┬нed by the
body:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ani┬нmat┬нed short above, The Dot and the Line, direct┬нed by the great Chuck Jones and nar┬нrat┬нed by Eng┬нlish actor Robert Mor┬нley, won an Oscar in 19656 for Best Ani┬нmat┬нed Short Film. Based on a book writ┬нten┬аby Nor┬нton Juster,┬атАЬThe Dot and the LineтАЭ tells the sto┬нry of a romance between two geo┬нmet┬нric shapesтАФtaking the arche┬нtyp┬нal nar┬нra┬нtive tra┬нjec┬нto┬нry of boy meets girl, los┬нes girl, wins girl in the end (find┬нing him┬нself along the way) and inject┬нing it with some fas┬нci┬нnat┬нing social com┬нmen┬нtary that still res┬нonates almost fifty years lat┬нer. One way of watch┬нing тАЬThe Dot and the LineтАЭ is as a тАЬtri┬нumph of the nerdтАЭ sto┬нry, where an anx┬нious square (as in тАЬuncoolтАЭ) Line has to com┬нpete with a hip┬нster beat┬нnik Squig┬нgle of a rival for the affec┬нtions of a flighty Dot.
The Line begins the film тАЬstiff as a stickтАж dull, con┬нven┬нtion┬нal and repressedтАЭ (as his love inter┬нest says of him) in con┬нtrast to the groovy Squig┬нgle and his groovy bebop sound┬нtrack. With the pos┬нsi┬нble sug┬нges┬нtion that this love trans┬нgress┬нes mid-cen┬нtu┬нry racial bound┬нaries, the LineтАЩs friends dis┬нap┬нprove and tell him to give it up, since тАЬthey all look alike any┬нway.тАЭ But the Line per┬нsists in his fol┬нly, indulging in some Wal┬нter Mit┬нty-like rever┬нies of hero┬нic endeav┬нors that might win over his Dot. Final┬нly, using тАЬgreat self-con┬нtrol,тАЭ he man┬нages to bend him┬нself into an angle, then anoth┬нer, then a series of sim┬нple, then very com┬нplex, shapes, becom┬нing, we might assume, some kind of math┬нe┬нmat┬нi┬нcal wiz. After refin┬нing his tal┬нents alone, he goes off to show them to Dot, who is тАЬover┬нwhelmedтАЭ and delight┬нed and who тАЬgig┬нgles like a school┬нgirl.тАЭ
Here the sub┬нtext of the nerd-gets-the-girl sto┬нry┬нline man┬нi┬нfests a fair┬нly con┬нser┬нv┬нa┬нtive cri┬нtique of the тАЬanar┬нchyтАЭ of the Squig┬нgle, whom the Dot comes to see as тАЬundis┬нci┬нplined, grace┬нless, coarseтАЭ and oth┬нer unflat┬нter┬нing adjec┬нtives while the lineтАФwho pro┬нclaimed to him┬нself ear┬нli┬нer that тАЬfree┬нdom is not a license for chaosтАЭтАФis тАЬdaz┬нzling, clever, mys┬нte┬нri┬нous, ver┬нsa┬нtile, light, elo┬нquent, pro┬нfound, enig┬нmat┬нic, com┬нplex, and com┬нpelling.тАЭ I can almost imag┬нine that George Will had a hand in the writ┬нing, which is to say that itтАЩs enor┬нmous┬нly clever, and enor┬нmous┬нly invest┬нed in the val┬нues of self-con┬нtrol, hard work, and dis┬нci┬нpline, and dis┬нtrust┬нful of spon┬нtane┬нity, free play, and gen┬нer┬нal groovi┬нness. At the end of the film, our Dot and Line go off to live тАЬif not hap┬нpi┬нly ever after, at least rea┬нson┬нably soтАЭ in some cozy sub┬нurb, no doubt. The moral of the sto┬нry? тАЬTo the vec┬нtor belong the spoils.тАЭ
If you would like to sup┬нport the mis┬нsion of Open Cul┬нture, con┬нsid┬нer mak┬нing┬аa dona┬нtion to our site. ItтАЩs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬нtri┬нbu┬нtions will help us con┬нtin┬нue pro┬нvid┬нing the best free cul┬нtur┬нal and edu┬нca┬нtion┬нal mate┬нri┬нals to learn┬нers every┬нwhere. You can con┬нtribute through Pay┬нPal, Patre┬нon, and Ven┬нmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬нed Con┬нtent┬а
Carl Sagan Explains Evo┬нlu┬нtion in an 8тАСMinute Ani┬нma┬нtion
Watch тАЬGeom┬нe┬нtry of Cir┬нcles,тАЭ the Abstract┬аSesame Street┬аAni┬нma┬нtion Scored by Philip Glass (1979)
Jour┬нney to the Cen┬нter of a Tri┬нan┬нgle: Watch the 1977 Dig┬нi┬нtal Ani┬нma┬нtion That Demys┬нti┬нfies Geom┬нe┬нtry
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬нcian based in Durham, NC.┬а

title: You Can Have Your Ashes Turned Into a Playable Vinyl Record, When Your Day Comes
description:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only lim┬нit┬нed by our imag┬нi┬нna┬нtion in how we want to go out. The
body:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only lim┬нit┬нed by our imag┬нi┬нna┬нtion in how we want to go out. There are now ways to turn our corpse into a tree, or have our ash┬нes shot into space, or press our ash┬нes into dia┬нmondsтАУI believe Super┬нman is involved in that last one. And now for the music lover, a com┬нpa┬нny called And Viny┬нly will press your ash┬нes into a playable vinyl record.
You like that pun┬нny com┬нpa┬нny name? ThereтАЩs more: the busi┬нness lets the dear depart┬нed тАЬLive on from beyond the groove.тАЭ Hear that groan? ThatтАЩs the deceased lit┬нer┬нal┬нly spin┬нning in their graveтАжon a turntable.
The UK-based com┬нpa┬нny has been around since 2009, when Jason Leach launched it тАЬjust for funтАЭ at first. But a lot of peo┬нple liked the idea and have kept him in busi┬нness.
It will cost, how┬нev┬нer. The basic ser┬нvice gen┬нer┬нal┬нly costs between ┬г1000 and ┬г3000 GBP, and it part┬нly depends on how many vinyl records you pro┬нduce. From what we can tell, you can┬нnot use copy┬нright-pro┬нtect┬нed music to fill up the 18тАУ22 min┬нutes per side. So no тАЬFree BirdтАЭ or тАЬWe Are the Cham┬нpi┬нons,тАЭ unfor┬нtu┬нnate┬нly. But you can put any┬нthing else: a voice record┬нing, or the sounds of nature, or com┬нplete silence. Get more infor┬нma┬нtion over at the com┬нpa┬нnyтАЩs FAQ.
No doubt, the ser┬нvice can pro┬нvide com┬нfort and a mem┬нo┬нry trig┬нger for those left behind. The above video, тАЬHear┬нing Madge,тАЭ is a short doc about a son who took record┬нings of his moth┬нer and used And Viny┬нly to make a record out of them. ItтАЩs sweet.
тАЬIтАЩm sure a lot of peo┬нple think that itтАЩs creepy, a lot of peo┬нple think itтАЩs sac┬нri┬нle┬нgious,тАЭ the man says. тАЬBut I know my moth┬нer wouldnтАЩt have. She wouldтАЩve thought it was a hoot.тАЭ
Jason Leach, a musi┬нcian and vinyl col┬нlec┬нtor him┬нself, talks of the imme┬нdi┬нa┬нcy of sound and what it means to many.
тАЬSound is vibrat┬нing you, the room, and itтАЩs actu┬нal┬нly mov┬нing the air around you,тАЭ he says. тАЬAnd thatтАЩs whatтАЩs so pow┬нer┬нful about hear┬нing someoneтАЩs voice on a record. TheyтАЩre actu┬нal┬нly mov┬нing the air and for me thatтАЩs pow┬нer┬нful.тАЭ
Note: An ear┬нli┬нer ver┬нsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
Relat┬нed Con┬нtent:
John CleeseтАЩs Eulo┬нgy for Gra┬нham Chap┬нman: тАШGood Rid┬нdance, the Free-Load┬нing Bas┬нtard, I Hope He FriesтАЩ
Bronze Age Britons Turned Bones of Dead Rel┬нa┬нtives into Musi┬нcal Instru┬нments & Orna┬нments
Watch Carl SaganтАЩs тАЬA Glo┬нri┬нous DawnтАЭ Become the First Vinyl Record Played in Space, Cour┬нtesy of Jack White
Death: A Free Online Phi┬нlos┬нo┬нphy Course from Yale Helps You Grap┬нple with the Inescapable
 Ted Mills is a free┬нlance writer on the arts.

title: Gijs Van Vaerenbergh Gracefully Reimagines a 16th-Century Belgian Abbey Church in Steel
description: In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few deca
body:
In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few decades, amid plenty of political tumult, Herkenrode Abbey in Hasselt, Belgium, was converted to the first Cistercian convent for women. It was a site of pilgrimage from the 13th to the 15th centuries, and despite regional wars and economic uncertainty, it stayed the course. During the 16th century, it experienced its heyday thanks to the patronage of a figure named Prince Bishop Evrard van der Marck, seeing the addition of a Gothic church that brimmed with beautiful stained glass windows, textiles, paintings, and more.
The Eighty YearsтАЩ War paused HerkenrodeтАЩs prosperity, and once things stabilized again politically, the abbey experienced several decades of good fortune, although much of this wealth was spent on the abbessesтАЩ own acquisitions of property and art in a show of their prestige. But the paradigm-shifting Li├иge Revolution, which coincided with the French Revolution, brought all of this crashing to a halt by 1796. The abbey complex was promptly sold and dismantled.

Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, Heckenrode went through many other uses, including factories and even a series of private homes, before it was again acquired by a religious organization in the 1970s. While the original 16th-century abbey church no longer exists due to a devastating fire in 1826, the site remains one of the regionтАЩs most culturally significant. And Herita has been working to restore it. As part of a phased regeneration of the landmark and its park, an ethereal, life-size sculpture of the abbey titled CLAUSURA by Gijs Van Vaerenbergh has risen from the buildingтАЩs original footprint.
The studio, founded by Belgian designers Pieterjan Gijs and Arnout Van Vaerenbergh, conceived of CLAUSURA as тАЬan artistic vision for the vanished heart of Herkenrode.тАЭ True to scale, the structure is made of slender steel rods that rise from the ground in an airy framework. The installation revolves around the idea of memory and sensation: rather than rebuilding the abbey to try to mirror what it may have looked like hundreds of years ago, the work nods to its past with an airy elegance. 
тАЬThe new volumes are transparent, allowing their silhouettes to blend seamlessly with the landscape in the background,тАЭ says a statement. тАЬThe intervention balances between reconstruction and abstraction, as the original structures are evoked through a refined play of suggestion. Iconic details such as windows, vaults, and towers enhance the sense of recognisability, although at times, these elements dissolve back into a chaos of lines.тАЭ

Gijs Van Vaerenbergh is known for its architectural interventions, often utilizing steel, stone, wood, and a wide range of other building materials to re-envision spaces as structural sculptures. тАЬWhat unites their diverse output is a sustained focus on┬аhow space is experiencedтАФvisually, bodily and temporally,тАЭ a statement says.
CLAUSURA is being constructed in three phases. The first, which is also the most ambitious, is slated to open to the public on June 18. Visitors will be able to walk and reflect amid the installation. See more on Gijs Van VaerenberghтАЩs Instagram, and learn about the restoration progress and how to visit on HeritaтАЩs website.

title: Vibrant Victorian-Era Transparencies Illuminate a Host of Microscopic Creatures
description: During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known
body:
During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known as the magic lantern, not to mention one in which eyeglasses became more affordable and entering the mainstream. These advances also influenced scientific inquiry, making microscopes more powerful, and the pursuit of microscopy enabled researchers and enthusiasts to discover creatures invisible to the naked eye.
One of these enthusiasts was London-based educator and amateur scientist Charles Thomas Hudson. Along with other scholars and aficionados, he participated in interest groups. тАЬAs President of the Royal Microscopical Society and a Fellow of the Royal Society, Hudson was a leading figure in this growing scientific community,тАЭ says a statement from Osh Gallery, which is currently exhibiting a collection of unique illustrations in The Hudson Transparencies.
Sea slugs
Curated by┬аLuke Powell and Jody Hudson-Powell of design firm Pentagram, which runs Osh Gallery, the exhibition literally brings to light a number of colorful transparencies that Hudson used during his lectures. While they appear dark and even unfinished in a typical setting, тАЬwhen lit from behind these intricate works transform into magical visions of life previously only glimpsed when viewed under a microscope,тАЭ the gallery says.
The Hudson Transparencies includes 58 original transparencies that measure a surprisingly large 37.8 by 29.5 inches. Each of the graphicsтАЩ proportions are тАЬthe equivalent of drawing ants the size of elephants,тАЭ says a statement. The animals and botanicals emerge through a combination of painted paper and perforations, which are made with lines and clusters of pinholes.
Hudson was particularly fascinated by rotifers, a phylum of zooplankton named for their so-called тАЬwheel-bearingтАЭ characteristics. He also catalogued algae, protozoa, and larvaeтАФsuch as that of the mayfly with its feather-like tail. Microscopic marine organisms were a particular favorite, though, and these back-lit images highlight the convergence of science and spectacular visuals that not only brought these creatures to life in a unique way for 19th-century viewers but continue to awe us today.
The Hudson Transparencies continues through June 11 in London. You might also enjoy Martin KunzтАЩs turn-of-the-century tactile graphics and Lorenz OkenтАЩs seminal natural history work,┬аAllgemaine Naturgeschichte F├╝r Alle St├дnde.
Opercularia nutans
A larva of a mayfly
A collection of rotifer species
Testudinella patina
Sponges, Porifera
Proales werneckii
Cupelopagis vorax

title: We Spent a Week Quarantined on an Uninhabited Island with 80 Artists
description: A muscular Englishman in a khaki kilt and black beret hops atop the edge of an old well clad in traditional Spanish tile, his sleeves rolled up to
body:
A muscular Englishman in a khaki kilt and black beret hops atop the edge of an old well clad in traditional Spanish tile, his sleeves rolled up to his elbows in what can only be called an act of bravery.┬а High winds and rain pelt a group of visitors from all directions, and yet, this charismatic performer stands tall above the cobblestone to announce that heтАЩs been living on this vacant island for nearly two centuries. HeтАЩs here to give us a tour.
тАЬThis has been my home for 174 years,тАЭ the man says, introducing himself as Captain Horatio Hollingwood. тАЬI arrived in command of a well-known British merchant ship, responsible for transporting goods of every sort. But alongside grain, wool, and oil, there travelled with us certain rather unwelcome companionsтАФterrible diseases. We stopped here for a sanitary inspection. And from here, I never left.тАЭ
Photo by Christopher Jobson
As our group endures the Balearic IslandsтАЩ mercurial spring weather and shivers among towering stone walls and outbuildings, this exuberant actor introduces us to the Lazaretto of Mah├│n, an 18th-century fortress and infirmary that once housed merchants, shipping crews, and any travelers seeking entry to Spain. His ability to rouse a group of studio artists into the turbulent outdoors is a fitting introduction to the activities of the week ahead. Alongside nearly 80 others from Slovakia to Argentina, Washington D.C. to Melbourne, weтАЩre here on this small, uninhabited island for Quarantine, a residency-style program conceived by artist Carles Gomila, who is determined to help artists break free from creative blocks while giving them permission to fail, discover, iterate, and hopefully, discover something new about themselves.┬а
For seven days, participants follow a rigorous schedule, arriving by boat on the island by 8:30 a.m. and leaving no earlier than 9:30 p.m. Their days are filled with talks, workshops, and meetings with invited artists who serve as mentors, the schedule of which isnтАЩt shared in advance. Phones, laptops, and any device with an internet connection are banned, and thereтАЩs no option to retreat to a hotel bed or wander off for an afternoon. Such a demanding and purposefully opaque schedule invites artists to settle into discomfort, abandon expectations, and confront the insecurities and anxieties capable of stifling their best work. The theme of this edition is Tears in Rain, which takes its name from the iconic monologue at the end of Ridley ScottтАЩs Blade Runner.
тАЬWhat I wish now is to share with you the story of the people who lived here over the past centuries,тАЭ the actor continues. тАЬYour quarantine, unlike theirs, is not compulsory. You have chosen to be here, to experience something meaningful in the way you live your lives and understand your creative process. This is a space and a time for transformation. Some passed here from life into death. Yours is a passage from blockage into freedom. Follow me.тАЭ
Getting to the island is no small featтАФour journey from Chicago took two days and required three flights, a car trip, a 15-minute walk, and a short boat rideтАФand thereтАЩs no open, public access to the lazaretto. Given its remote location and secret programming, Quarantine asks interested artists to apply on a true leap of faith, one that many describe as the first moment they had to relinquish control and believe the bold claims the program boasts. Testimonials include lofty statements about the organizers тАЬminting a legion for the revolutionтАЭ and how participants feel тАЬlike my insides have been blown out.тАЭ Some people even get the programтАЩs tally logo tattooed, and many have returned for multiple visits.
Photo by Christopher Jobson
If youтАЩre thinking this sounds like a cult, youтАЩre not alone. When QuarantineтАЩs organizers invited us to observe the April 2026 edition, we were skeptical, and so were the friends and colleagues with whom we shared our plans. As it turns out, many of the participants had similar reservations, which we learned when we landed in Menorca and met an artist at baggage claim. (In keeping with the spirit of Quarantine and the idea that what happens on the island stays on the island, weтАЩre only sharing information about participants anonymously.) He was coming from Los Angeles and first encountered the program through one of the sessionтАЩs mentors, Yuko Shimizu, whom heтАЩd long admired and previously collaborated with. Lured by the opportunity to untether from daily life and connect with professional artists, he hoped to reinvigorate his practice and figure out how to take the next step, something former Quarantine participants lauded and that he hoped he could access, too. Was it a cult, though? None of us was sure.
From the 18th to 20th century, the Spanish government required all travelers, no matter their origin, to sequester on the island for 40 days or if they fell ill, longer. These groups were cordoned off by their presumed and actual illnesses, and about five percent died during their stay, succumbing to infectious diseases like the Bubonic Plague and Yellow Fever. Today, the double-walled sanatorium is mid-restoration as the local government repairs crumbling limestone halls and terracotta walkways and trims back an abundance of thistles. Along with a handful of loquacious peacocks whose eerie calls echoed across the island, just a skeleton grounds crew and the occasional tour group occupy the island with any regularity.
Quarantine is one of two recurring events held on the lazaretto, with weeklong editions each April and October that are supported by the local government and local tourism organization, Fundaci├│ Foment del Turisme de Menorca. Nearly everything needed for the program must be loaded onto boats and carried to the island for every edition, and a local caterer packs food for 80 and traverses the harbor each lunch and dinner. Enormous musical instruments like the bilasтАФa rare, standing contraption of flat bells conceived by Russian Alexander ZhikharevтАФeven make their way over for live, outdoor performances.
A sort of mystical bootcamp for artists, Quarantine is both intensely communal and unabashedly introspective. Gomila designs the workshop sessions, known as the тАЬArt Lab,тАЭ to tap into as many emotions and responses as possible, often frustration, confusion, and eventually, clarity. Many incorporate music, and almost all center on life drawing, whether through self-portraiture or enthusiastic models who embrace the spirit of the project as much as the participants. They don costumes, hold sabres as props, and accessorize to an outlandish extent. Models are invited to share in the creative process, too, and as one tells us one evening over glasses of Cava, the program allows her to reconnect with the self she doesnтАЩt always encounter in her life as an architect.
Photo by Christopher Jobson
Everyone we meet at Quarantine echoes this sentiment, whether theyтАЩre full-time artists or not. ThereтАЩs a young father whose work at a video game design studio is forcing him to rely more and more on A.I. A fine art educator laments the corporatization of her position as a faculty member at a for-profit university. And countless others who work in tech, finance, government, design, and illustration have ventured to the Mediterranean to reclaim focus, hone their voice, and if theyтАЩre lucky, make something that excites them.
The accomplished group of mentors doesnтАЩt hurt either. AprilтАЩs edition included Shimizu, Martin Wittfooth, Mu Pan, Phil Hale, Yulia Bas, Sean Layh, and Adam Miller, while past sessions featured Miles Johnston, Jeremy Mann, and Nicol├бs Uribe, to name a few. Mentors each present a morning masterclass on a wide range of topics, from WittfoothтАЩs concept of art as a тАЬspirit artifactтАЭ to ShimizuтАЩs courage in changing careers after a decade in a corporate job. Layh shares his story of picking up his paintbrush for the first time in more than a decade to re-learn his abilities over two and a half years on a single canvas (last month he won an Archibald Prize). Participants also receive one-on-one sessions with three mentors, in which no topics are off limits. They can ask for guidance in developing a particular technique, although most choose to utilize their 45-minute sessions to chat about more personal problems theyтАЩve both faced and connect about what it means to be an artist in todayтАЩs world.┬а
This equalizing ethos is the foundation of Quarantine. When participants complete an exercise, all work is displayed on a central table, and if theyтАЩd like, they can share something with the group. ThereтАЩs no critique, no comparison, and no need to explain why they made the decisions they did. The focus instead is on the process, on seizing moments of low-risk spontaneity. Experimentation and abandoning patterns that no longer serve their creativity are encouraged, along with developing practices to work through frustrations and insecurities. The wide range of skills is liberating: many artists have worked full-time for more than a decade, while others are painting with oils for the very first time.
тАЬWhat happens here is so psychological,тАЭ shares one participant from Argentina who heard about Quarantine by following Layh. тАЬBecause itтАЩs all so mysterious, I was worried it was going to be cheesy, but IтАЩve cried three times this week.тАЭ
Photo by Romas Tauras
On the final day, after participants have painted and sketched for dozens of hours, been subjected to creative exercises they hope to never encounter again and others they will gladly replicate at home, and let themselves be vulnerable in a way that rarely happens outside a therapistтАЩs office, what seems to stand out is the camaraderie and an overwhelming sense of belonging. In comparison to the eager anxieties of the first day, the group has settled into a shared clarity, knowing not to fear mistakes and feeling a new sense of kinship among like-minded peers. They pair off to get coffee, encourage one another to try a strange technique, and make plans to meet up once they return home. We were told that WhatsApp group chats from previous editions continue to this day. A large contingent from a previous year also wants to return en masse.┬а
The last evening under a star-studded sky, unusually visible to us city dwellers, a fire pit appeared adjacent to the well that the Englishman jumped atop on day one. All 80 of us gathered around, and one mentor, Bas, kicked us off. In her hands were an old letter that once held significant weight in her life and a work on paper. She walked over to the fire and tossed both in, then asked everyone else to do the same.┬а
As the fire pit grew so full of paintings and drawings and sketches and notes that pieces spilled onto the cobblestone, the communal sense of catharsis and release was palpable. Artists danced hand in hand, cried, hugged, and stood solemnly watching their breakthroughs crumble into ash. The idea, of course, was that these material objectsтАУthese тАЬspirit artifactsтАЭ in WittfoothтАЩs parlanceтАУwere just that: artifacts. Artworks made on the island were both irreplaceable and irrelevant, as the program had already built up a herd immunity to any sense of assuredness or control. What Quarantine offers instead is a shared pathology, one that focuses not on remedying the symptoms of creative blocks or failures but rather zeroes in on the underlying cause.
Photo by Christopher Jobson
Photo by Romas Tauras
Photo by Romas Tauras

schema.headline: We Spent a Week Quarantined on an Uninhabited Island with 80 Artists

title: Iran Will Not Participate in Venice Biennale, Organizers Say
description:
News
                The news of IranтАЩs exit from this yearтАЩs edition comes two months after the US and Israel first launched strikes
body:
News
                The news of IranтАЩs exit from this yearтАЩs edition comes two months after the US and Israel first launched strikes across the country. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Iran's pavilion exhibition for the 58th Venice Biennale in 2019, Of being and singing, with works by Reza Lavassani, Samira Alikhanzadeh, and Ali Meer Azimi (photo by Simone Padovani/Awakening/Getty Images)
    
        
            With a single day remaining before the 61st Venice Biennale opens for previews this week, the Biennale Foundation announced today that Iran will not be participating in the national pavilion exhibition. The news comes two months after the United States and Israel┬аfirst launched strikes across Iran, and coincides with resumed attacks along the Strait of Hormuz as tensions throughout the temporary ceasefire bubbled over in the last 24 hours. Little information is available about the pavilion exhibition aside from the fact that Aydin Mehdizadeh Tehrani, the director general of the visual arts office in the Iranian Ministry of Culture, was listed as the commissioner as of March 4. тАЬWith regard to the National Participations in the 61st International Art Exhibition, In Minor Keys by Koyo Kouoh (9 May тАУ 22 November 2026), it has been announced that the┬аIslamic Republic of Iran┬аwill not participate,тАЭ a May 4 press release on the Biennale's website simply reads. The statement notes that the event now comprises 100 national participants, including the United Republic of Tanzania and the Republic of Seychelles, which were added in March.Hyperallergic has reached out to the Biennale Foundation and Mehdizadeh Tehrani for comment. Without owning a pavilion in the Giardini, Iran has had a spotty attendance at the Venice Biennale since 1958. Though there was a marked absence between the 1960s and 2003 attributed to the lead-up to and aftermath of the 1979 Islamic Revolution, Iran has mounted national exhibitions at the Biennale semi-consistently throughout the last two decades тАФ most recently participating in the 2024 edition. The 2026 Venice Biennale has drawn criticism for its inclusion of both Russia and Israel, with cultural figures around the globe pledging boycotts and protest actions. Hundreds of artists, curators, and staff participating in the 61st edition signed letters calling on organizers to exclude Israel over its ongoing genocide in Palestine. The Biennale awards jury recently resigned en masse in tune with its initial decision to omit Israel and Russia from consideration for the Golden Lion awards, prompting the Foundation to institute the тАЬVisitor Lions,тАЭ prizes awarded by public vote at the end of the Biennale, last week.
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        Venice Biennale
                      
                      
                        Iran
                      
                      
                        2026 Iran War

schema.headline: Iran Will Not Participate in Venice Biennale, Organizers Say

title: Met Gala Boycott Message Projected on BezosтАЩs Manhattan Penthouse
description:
News
                "All working-class people deserve better than what we're getting,тАЭ a former Amazon worker told Hyperallergi
body:
News
                "All working-class people deserve better than what we're getting,тАЭ a former Amazon worker told Hyperallergic ahead of this yearтАЩs gala, co-chaired by Jeff Bezos.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            A guerrilla projection on Jeff Bezos's luxury Madison Square Park residence on May 3, 2026 (all photos Isa Farfan/Hyperallergic)On the eve of the Met Gala in New York City, a series of messages condemning Jeff Bezos and Amazon lit up the billionaire's Madison Square Park luxury residence on Sunday, May 3.The guerrilla projection is the latest in the group Everyone Hates Elon's protest campaign against Bezos's involvement in Monday's event. After the Costume Institute at the Metropolitan Museum of Art named Bezos and his wife Lauren Sachez honorary co-chairs of the gala in February, the group has denounced Amazon's alleged dangerous workplace conditions and the company's lucrative contracts with federal immigration agencies through a series of visual actions.The group hid "piss bottles" in a Bezos protest ahead of the Met Gala. (screenshot via @everyonehateselon_ on Instagram)Last week, the group littered the Met with bottles of fake urine in a reference to claims from Amazon delivery workers that they were forced to pee in plastic bottles under severe time constraints.  The group also wheatpasted posters across the city that called for a boycott of the event. A video cast onto Bezos's penthouse on Sunday evening featured the testimony of Mary Hill, a 72-year-old Amazon warehouse worker who says she struggles to live paycheck to paycheck while battling cancer. Hill has been at the forefront of a campaign to secure better conditions for elder Amazon employees. In the video projection, she called for the Met Gala to honor the company's workers instead of the billionaire. "These are the people who should be honored at big events, not people who avoid their taxes and help Donald Trump,тАЭ an organizer for Everyone Hates Elon told Hyperallergic.A testimony by Amazon warehouse employee Mary Hill played against the exterior of the luxury building where Bezos's owns a penthouse Everyone Hates Elon also projected slogans, including "Boycott The Bezos Met Gala," onto the residence and, later, onto the Chrysler and Empire State buildings."тАКThe stories [the Amazon workers] have are incredibly powerful, and these are the people we should be celebrating, billionaires," an organizer for Everyone Hates Elon told Hyperallergic. "These are the people who should be honored at big events, not people who avoid their taxes and help Donald Trump." Passersby sit near Bezos's apartment building.Nearby, a small group of union workers, including Lamont Hopewell, who formerly worked in an Amazon warehouse in Queens, gathered to commemorate the projection. тАЬAll working-class people deserve better than what we're getting, especially workers at Amazon; that's the main reason we're here," Hopewell told Hyperallergic. Hopewell learned of the projection through his union, Teamsters Local 804.тАК"It makes me feel inspired. It makes me feel like what we're doing is the right thing," Hopewell said, reacting to the display. "People are actually on board with us, even if they're not part of the organization; they still feel the same sentiment that we have."
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New York
                      
                      
                        Met Gala

                        Metropolitan Museum of Art
                      
                      
                        Jeff Bezos
